description: Public-domain MIDI catalog for guitar / piano voice testing. Each entry is a single Standard MIDI File downloaded from the Mutopia Project, redistributable under Public Domain. Rendered audio output (WAV) is gitignored — generate per-engine renders with `cargo run --release --bin render_midi --features native -- --in bench-out/songs/<name>.mid --engine <ENGINE> --out <OUT>.wav`.

license_summary.verification: Mutopia Project entries verified via mutopiaproject.org catalog (Public Domain / CC-BY only — ShareAlike and NonCommercial rejected). CC0 entries from github.com/m-malandro/CC0-midis (LICENSE = CC0 1.0 Universal, verified 2026-04-29). All URLs HEAD-checked before download.

entries.title: D. Aguado — Op. 3 No. 1 (Estudio, 1820s)
entries.context: 19th-century pedagogy: open-string root chords plus step-wise melodic motion. Short, consistent — good A/B regression candidate when comparing guitar-voice changes.

entries.title: J.S. Bach — BWV 999 Prelude in D minor (lute, c. 1720)
entries.context: Every classical guitarist's first Bach: broken-chord arpeggios at moderate tempo with sustained pedal tones. Tests how cleanly overlapping plucks ring against each other.

entries.title: M. Carcassi — Op. 1 No. 1 (1820s)
entries.context: Carcassi études are the standard intermediate repertoire — right-hand finger patterns drill arpeggio voicings at clean tempo.

entries.title: M. Giuliani — Op. 50 No. 1 'Le Papillon' (1822)
entries.context: Light melodic figuration over staccato bass. Tests crisp note-onset behaviour at low velocities — the regime where most physical models start to fall apart.

entries.title: F. Sor — Op. 1 No. 1 (Six Petites Pièces, 1810s)
entries.context: Sor is to classical guitar what Czerny is to piano — these miniatures are the foundational étude literature.

entries.title: F. Tárrega — Adelita (Mazurka, 1899)
entries.context: Slow held melody notes over arpeggio accompaniment. Directly tests sustain envelope shape on quarter and half notes.

entries.title: F. Tárrega — Capricho Árabe (1892)
entries.context: Phrygian-flavoured solo with rubato passages. Tests pluck-attack consistency under rhythmic flexibility rather than steady tempo.

entries.title: F. Tárrega — Recuerdos de la Alhambra (1896)
entries.context: The canonical tremolo study: repeated 16th notes on a single melodic pitch. Any micro-decay or amplitude-modulation artefact in a guitar voice surfaces immediately.

entries.title: Traditional — Red Apple Rag
entries.context: Early-20th-century American ragtime arranged for solo guitar. Bright major-key syncopation — escapes the medieval / classical-MIDI vibe of the rest of the catalogue.

entries.title: Traditional — Soldier's Joy
entries.context: Canonical bluegrass / fiddle hoedown tune in D major. Fast eighth-note runs, tests note-onset at speed.

entries.title: W.A. Mozart — Piano Sonata KV 309, 1st movement (1777)
entries.context: Classical-period Allegro con spirito with broad dynamic range and full keyboard coverage. Stresses register balance and onset transients across the piano voice.

entries.title: E. Satie — Danse (from 6 Croquis et agaceries, 1913)
entries.context: Satie's spare harmonic language and slow tempo expose any envelope or sustain artefact that a busier romantic piece would mask.

entries.title: I. Albéniz — Op. 71 Rumores de la Caleta (1886)
entries.context: Spanish-romantic Andalusian flavour with rolled chords and rubato. Tests the piano voice on a programmatic / coloristic piece rather than a strict-tempo classical line.

entries.title: Das Wohltemperierte Klavier I, Praeludium I
entries.context: Baroque-period Bach guitar piece (BWV 846) composed 1722 — exercises the guitar-stk voice on real repertoire from the Mutopia catalog.

entries.title: Sonata No. 8 “Pathétique” (2nd Movement: Adagio cantabile)
entries.context: Classical-period Beethoven guitar piece (Op. 13) composed 1798 — exercises the guitar-stk voice on real repertoire from the Mutopia catalog.

entries.title: S. Joplin — The Entertainer (1902)
entries.context: Canonical American ragtime — syncopated right-hand melody over steady stride bass. Stage E genre breadth: pulls jukebox out of pure-classical bias.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Elite Syncopations (1902)
entries.context: Mid-tempo Joplin ragtime — alternating bass with sixteenth-note cross-rhythms in the right hand.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Pineapple Rag (1908)
entries.context: Late-period Joplin — chromatic inner-voice motion stresses voice-leading clarity at piano-roll tempi.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Something Doing (1903)
entries.context: Co-written with Scott Hayden. Bouncier ragtime, lots of breaks — useful counterpoint to the more uniform Entertainer.

entries.title: S. Joplin — The Strenuous Life (1902)
entries.context: Roosevelt-era Joplin rag, named after T. Roosevelt's speech. Driving stride bass.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Wall Street Rag (1909)
entries.context: Programmatic Joplin: each section has a written caption (panic, good times, etc.). Tests dynamic-range range-mapping.

entries.title: S. Joplin — A Breeze From Alabama (1902)
entries.context: Brisk march-rag hybrid. Demonstrates Joplin's debt to Sousa-era march form before settling into rag syncopation.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Bethena: A Concert Waltz (1905)
entries.context: Joplin in 3/4 — slow concert waltz, not a rag. Shows the ragtime voice can sustain non-syncopated material too.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Eugenia (1905)
entries.context: Mid-period Joplin rag, classic AABBACCDD form.

entries.title: S. Joplin — Magnetic Rag (1914)
entries.context: Joplin's last published rag. Minor-key middle section is unusual for the genre — useful tonal stress test.

entries.title: Trad. (Finnish) — Aamulla varhain
entries.context: Finnish folk song with piano accompaniment. Adds a Nordic timbre into the catalog's geographic mix.

entries.title: Trad. (Japanese) — Banza-Bushi
entries.composer: Traditional (Japanese) — arr. Y. Nagai, K. Obata
entries.context: Edo-period min'yō melody transcribed for shamisen. Pentatonic, sparse texture — exposes voice attack envelope on isolated plucks.

entries.title: Trad. (Japanese) — Yanreso Hoi
entries.composer: Traditional (Japanese) — arr. Y. Nagai, K. Obata
entries.context: Japanese work-song min'yō for shamisen. Modal — useful counterpoint to the diatonic-major bias of the European catalog.

entries.title: Trad. (Japanese) — Noue Bushi
entries.composer: Traditional (Japanese) — arr. Y. Nagai, K. Obata
entries.context: Short Japanese folk piece for shamisen, ~30 s. Compact regression candidate.

entries.title: Trad. (Japanese) — Umewa Saitaka
entries.composer: Traditional (Japanese) — arr. Y. Nagai, K. Obata
entries.context: Japanese plum-blossom theme, shamisen transcription. Cherry-blossom-season programming hook for jukebox.

entries.title: Trad. (Japanese) — Doujan
entries.composer: Traditional (Japanese) — arr. Y. Nagai, K. Obata
entries.context: Short shamisen min'yō, in & yo modal mix. Geographically diverse from the Mutopia classical mainline.

entries.title: Trad. (Japanese) — Saakorasa
entries.composer: Traditional (Japanese) — arr. Y. Nagai, K. Obata
entries.context: Japanese folk shamisen piece. Tiny SMF (<1 KiB) but musically intact — handy stress test for short-piece UI rendering.

entries.title: Trad. (English) — Greensleeves (accordion arr.)
entries.context: Accordion arrangement of the canonical English Renaissance folk song. Companion to the existing lute Greensleaves.mid.

entries.title: J. P. Sousa — El Capitán March (1896)
entries.context: Sousa march drawn from his comic opera. Bright, brass-band character — adds American military-march era to the catalog.

entries.title: J. P. Sousa — Hail to the Spirit of Liberty (1900)
entries.context: Sousa march. Uniform marching-band tempo — useful steady-state regression sample.

entries.title: J. P. Sousa — Hands Across the Sea (1899)
entries.context: Sousa march dedicated to international friendship. Strong dynamic contrasts in the trio section.

entries.title: J. P. Sousa — King Cotton March (1895)
entries.context: Atlanta Cotton States Exposition march. Long form ABAC + trio — exercises voice across many sectional repeats.

entries.title: J. P. Sousa — The Liberty Bell March (1893)
entries.context: Famous internationally as the Monty Python theme. Bell-imitation chimes in the trio — useful percussion-adjacent texture.

entries.title: J. P. Sousa — The Stars and Stripes Forever (1896)
entries.context: Official US national march. Iconic trio piccolo countermelody — multi-voice density stress.

entries.title: S. C. Foster — Slumber, My Darling (1862)
entries.context: American 19th-century parlor lullaby — the genre that dominated US sheet-music sales pre-ragtime. Foster is the canonical name; predates Joplin by a generation.

entries.title: Anon. — Nuż my dziś krześcijani (Polish Christmas Carol)
entries.context: Polish Renaissance Christmas carol. Era override = Renaissance because composer string lacks a year range derive_era can parse.

entries.title: Anon. — Old 100th (original 1561 version)
entries.context: Genevan Psalter hymn tune that became 'All People That on Earth Do Dwell' / 'Doxology'. The original syncopated rhythm before later regularization.

entries.title: Anon. — Il Est de Bonne Heure Né
entries.context: Anonymous French Renaissance song. Modal melody — exercises voice in non-major-key repertoire.

entries.title: Anon. — Ich ruf zu dir, Herr Jesus Christ
entries.context: Lutheran chorale melody (later harmonized by Bach as BWV 639). Plain unharmonized version — useful as the source-tune A/B against Bach's chorale settings.

entries.title: Anon. — Lobt Gott, ihr Christen, allzugleich
entries.context: Lutheran chorale melody. Era override = Renaissance to keep it out of the Modern bucket.

entries.title: m-malandro — Frantic Boss Battle
entries.context: CC0 indie-game-style boss battle theme — fast 16th-note runs across multiple instruments. Exercises voice polyphony at high event density.

entries.title: m-malandro — Lighthearted Battle Theme
entries.context: Upbeat JRPG-style battle theme. Major-key, melodic — counterpoint to the frantic boss track.

entries.title: m-malandro — Arena Rock
entries.context: CC0 driving rock-arena instrumental. Power-chord harmonic vocabulary — extends genre coverage beyond keyboard literature.

entries.title: m-malandro — Game Over
entries.context: Short 'game over' sting (<1 KiB SMF). Useful as a UX-style cue and as a smoke-test for very short pieces.

entries.title: m-malandro — Gather Your Party
entries.context: Adventure-game tavern / party-formation cue. Mid-tempo, layered — a different timbral target than the battle tracks.

entries.title: m-malandro — Overture 2021
entries.context: Long-form (~44 KiB SMF) game overture. Tests sustained voice behavior over multi-minute playback.

entries.title: m-malandro — Sitar Jam
entries.context: CC0 indie game cue with sitar-flavored melodic line. Modal, pentatonic-leaning — non-Western tonal stress test.

entries.title: m-malandro — Math Metal
entries.context: Odd-meter math-metal-style instrumental. Irregular phrase lengths exercise the voice's transient response under unsteady accent patterns.

entries.title: m-malandro — Movin' On
entries.context: Mid-tempo CC0 instrumental — overworld-traversal vibe. Typical SNES-RPG-adjacent style.

entries.title: m-malandro — Android Observation Room
entries.context: Sci-fi ambient cue. Sparse texture — exposes voice noise floor / sustain quality.
